description: This figure presents the effect of aluminum content (3–10 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels after water quenching from 1150°C and aging at 550°C for 16 h. The chart comprises three stacked subplots: (1) upper panel shows mechanical strength versus Al content, plotting ultimate tensile strength (UTS, filled triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, open triangles) for two carbon levels—0.85% C (dashed lines) and 0.95% C (solid lines); (2) middle panel displays ductility indicators including reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, filled circles) versus Al content; (3) lower panel illustrates U-notch impact toughness (K_CU, diamonds) in J/cm² versus Al content. Key trends reveal that both UTS and YS remain relatively constant below ~7% Al but increase sharply thereafter, with higher carbon content consistently producing greater strength. Conversely, ductility (ψ and δ) gradually decreases with increasing Al. Impact toughness peaks at approximately 6–7% Al (~180 J/cm² for 0.85% C and ~160 J/cm² for 0.95% C) before declining significantly at higher Al contents. These data demonstrate the trade-off between strength and toughness, indicating that excessive Al (>7%) promotes precipitation hardening (likely κ-phase) that enhances strength but severely compromises impact resistance and ductility.
